name: best_friends
text: Bob moved to Amy's street when they were both seven. She was the one who walked up to the moving truck and asked if the family had any kids. They were in the same second grade class at Elm Ridge Elementary, and they sat next to each other because of alphabetical seating — Amy Chen, Bob Dawson. They started walking to school together every morning, and that just never stopped, even through middle school when most of their other friendships reshuffled. They both ran cross country in high school, both worked summers at the same pool — Amy as a lifeguard, Bob at the snack bar. They went to different colleges but texted every day and drove to visit each other at least once a month. Now they live about fifteen minutes apart and still get breakfast every Sunday.

name: bobs_unrequited_love
text: Bob learned how to make Amy's coffee order — oat milk latte, extra shot, a little cinnamon — before he learned how to make his own. He asked her out for the first time junior year of college, after they'd been studying together for organic chemistry all semester. He'd actually rehearsed it in his car beforehand. She said no and told him she liked things the way they were. He kept making her coffee the next morning like nothing happened. The second time was at a mutual friend's wedding two years later. He'd written something on a napkin, crossed it out, written it again, and then just said it out loud instead. She said no again, and they didn't talk for about three months after that. During those three months, Bob still checked her Instagram every day and almost called her eleven times — he knows because he counted the almost-calls in his notes app and then deleted the note. They eventually started hanging out again, but Bob never brought it up a third time. He did, however, keep a photo of the two of them from college pinned to his fridge. Amy started dating Marcus about six months ago. He's an architect. Bob has met him four times now, and Marcus always offers to buy him a beer. Bob always says yes and always finishes it too fast. He still makes two coffees every Sunday morning out of habit before remembering she doesn't come over anymore.

name: amy_acting_weird
text: Amy used to reply to texts within minutes. Now it takes her a day, sometimes two, and the responses are short — a word or two, no follow-up questions. She skipped Sunday breakfast three weeks in a row, which she's never done, not even when she had the flu last January and showed up in a blanket. Bob also noticed she stopped posting on Instagram entirely, and her car has been parked in the same spot every time he drives past her apartment. Last week he ran into her at the grocery store and she was buying frozen dinners, which is strange for someone who used to meal-prep every weekend and send him photos of her containers lined up in the fridge.
